text.p: SIR: In their zeal to govern Anambra State, two governorship candidates in the February 6 election, Dr. Chris Ngige of the Action Congress (AC) and Prof. Charles Soludo of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), have turned mathematics, which is an exact science, into politics, which is not science in the real sense. But two plus two must be four, no matter how the two previous candidates went to juggle figures politically or emotionally.

Ngige and Soludo claim that Governor Peter Obi did not make the statutory 25 per cent of votes cast in two-thirds of the 21 local councils in the State, as contained in the electoral law. Ngige said Obi won 25 per cent in only 13 local councils, instead of 14 as required by the law. Countering the claim, however, the INEC Resident Electoral Commissioner, Mr. Josiah Uwazuruonye, insists Obi actually scored 25 per cent of votes cast in 15 local councils.

A copy of the detailed results of the governorship election, issued by INEC, shows that though 301,232 votes were cast at the election, 16,685 were rejected, invalidated or voided, leaving only 284,547 as valid votes. Therefore, the percentage of votes scored by Obi or any other candidate was rightly and legally based on valid votes of 284,547 cast, and not on 301,232. In this way, INEC rightly calculated that Governor Obi won 25 per cent in 15 local councils, and therefore triumphed in the election.

If Ngige wants to go to the electoral tribunal, jolly well good, let him do so. It is his right. Indeed, tribunals and law courts exist to ensure that everyone stays within the rules. They are bulwarks of liberty, champions of the individual against abuses of power by scheming politicians, arrogant bureaucrats and the emotional excesses of transient majorities.

Maybe Ngige believes mathematics is politics, but surely, he cannot convince the tribunal so. Perhaps in realization of the futility of his case, he has forged an alliance with a group known as "Association of Disenfranchised Voters", who armed with fuel and lighter, and loaded in buses, were heading to the INEC office in Awka. It is a reminder of Ngige’s inglorious governorship tenure when Anambra was reduced to rubbles by hired thugs.

Anambra people will not allow this to happen again in our state. Under Governor Obi, in the past four years, we have been enjoying abundant peace and comfort. Luckily, too, the last concluded election was peaceful, free and fair.
